problem.title: Minimum Depth of Binary Tree
problem.description: Given a binary tree, find its minimum depth. The minimum depth is the number of nodes along the shortest path from the root node down to the nearest leaf node.
assumptions: Minimum depth = shortest path from root to any leaf.

example.explanation: The only leaf is node 6 at depth 5. BFS finds the shallowest leaf.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – BFS [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use BFS (level-order traversal) and return the depth of the first leaf node encountered. Because BFS explores nodes level by level, the very first leaf found is guaranteed to be at minimum depth. This is more efficient than DFS for wide trees because BFS stops as soon as the first leaf is reached.

solutions.title: Solution 2 – DFS Recursive [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Recursively compute the minimum depth. For a null node, return 0. For a leaf node (no children), return 1. If only the left child exists, return 1 + minDepth(left) — we must not count the missing right child as a depth-1 leaf. Similarly if only the right child exists. If both children exist, return 1 + min(minDepth(left), minDepth(right)). O(n) time O(H) space.
